response:
We need to extract structured data matching schema.

Fields:
- show: string. From text: "Show: The Changelog #587". Likely show name is "The Changelog". Episode number is 587. So show = "The Changelog". episode_number = 587.

- title: string. Title line: "Title: Why SQLite is taking over the world". So title = "Why SQLite is taking over the world".

- guest: object with name and role. Guest line: "Guest: Dr. Richard Hipp (SQLite creator)". So name = "Dr. Richard Hipp". role = "SQLite creator". Could also be "SQLite creator". We'll set role accordingly.

- recorded: string. Recorded line: "Recorded: March 12, 2024 | Duration: 1h 23m". So recorded = "March 12, 2024". (We ignore duration part.)

- duration_minutes: number. Duration: 1h 23m = 1*60 + 23 = 83 minutes.

- downloads_first_week: number. "Downloads (first week): 47,291". Remove comma: 47291.

- chapters: array of objects with timestamp and title. From timestamps list:

00:00 - Intro
03:45 - How SQLite started as a Tcl extension
12:30 - The testing philosophy (100% MC/DC coverage)
28:15 - SQLite in unexpected places (phones, browsers, aircraft)
45:00 - WASM compilation and running in the browser
58:30 - Competing with PostgreSQL?
1:12:00 - Future plans and the SQLite consortium
1:20:00 - Outro

We need timestamp strings exactly as given? Probably keep as "00:00", "03:45", etc. For 1:12:00 and 1:20:00, they are hour:minute:second? Actually they are 1:12:00 meaning 1 hour 12 minutes? The format is h:mm:ss? But earlier timestamps are mm:ss. For consistency, we can keep as given: "1:12:00", "1:20:00". Or maybe convert to "01:12:00"? But schema expects string; we can keep as given.

- sponsors: array of strings: "Fly.io, Sentry, Retool". So ["Fly.io", "Sentry", "Retool"].

Now produce JSON.

Check types: episode_number number, downloads_first_week number, duration_minutes number.

Make sure no trailing commas.

Return only JSON.
